title: Orthogonality & quadratic forms
summary: Inner products and orthonormal bases, the Gram-Schmidt algorithm, orthogonal matrices, the Spectral Theorem for real symmetric matrices, classifying quadratic forms by eigenvalue signs, and Cayley-Hamilton for powers and inverses.
objectives: State the Spectral Theorem and explain why real symmetric matrices are always orthogonally diagonalizable
Verify when a matrix is orthogonal and use the column/row orthonormality and det = +/-1 properties
Orthogonally diagonalize a symmetric matrix: find eigenvalues, orthogonalize eigenvectors, build the orthogonal P
Run the Gram-Schmidt algorithm to produce an orthonormal basis of a span or solution space
Write the symmetric matrix of a quadratic form and classify its signature (definite/indefinite/semidefinite) from eigenvalue signs
Apply the Cayley-Hamilton theorem to express powers and the inverse of a matrix as low-degree polynomials in the matrix

blocks.text:
This topic ties together three closely related ideas from lectures 21-22 (plus the quadratic-form classification of lecture 23). The central object is the **symmetric matrix** $S$ (one with $S^\top = S$). Symmetric matrices are the best-behaved matrices in the whole course: their eigenvalues are always real, eigenvectors from different eigenvalues are automatically orthogonal, and they can always be diagonalized by an **orthogonal** matrix. This is the **Spectral Theorem**.

From there we get the practical machinery: building **orthonormal (ON) bases** with the **Gram-Schmidt algorithm**, and using the signs of eigenvalues to classify **quadratic forms** as positive/negative definite, indefinite, or semidefinite.

For column vectors $v,w\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$ the **dot product** is $v\cdot w = v^\top w$, and the **norm** is $\|v\|=\sqrt{v\cdot v}$.

A basis $\{v_1,\dots,v_m\}$ of a subspace $V\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ is **orthonormal (ON)** if:
- $v_i\cdot v_i = 1$ for all $i$ (unit length),
- $v_i\cdot v_j = 0$ for all $i\neq j$ (mutually orthogonal).

**Pairwise orthogonal nonzero vectors are automatically linearly independent.** If $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_mv_m=0$, dotting with $v_j$ gives $a_j(v_j\cdot v_j)=0$, and since $v_j\neq 0$ we get $a_j=0$. So orthogonality is a free certificate of independence — you never have to row-reduce.

A matrix $P\in\mathbb{R}^{n,n}$ is **orthogonal** if any one (hence all) of these equivalent conditions holds:
- $P^\top P = I_n$,
- $PP^\top = I_n$,
- $P$ is invertible and $P^{-1}=P^\top$.

Condition $P^\top P=I_n$ says exactly that the **columns of $P$ are an orthonormal basis** of $\mathbb{R}^n$; $PP^\top=I_n$ says the **rows** are. So orthogonal $\iff$ rows (and columns) form an ON basis.

blocks.caption: From $1=\det I = \det(P^\top P)=(\det P)^2$ via Binet's theorem. Determinant $+1$ matrices are rotations; $-1$ are reflections/improper.

blocks.text:
**Lemma (orthogonal eigenvectors).** If $v_1,v_2$ are eigenvectors of a symmetric $S$ for distinct eigenvalues $\lambda_1\neq\lambda_2$, then $v_1\cdot v_2=0$.

Proof idea: $(Sv_1)\cdot v_2 = v_1^\top S^\top v_2 = v_1^\top(Sv_2)=v_1\cdot(Sv_2)$ because $S^\top=S$. So $\lambda_1\,v_1\cdot v_2 = \lambda_2\,v_1\cdot v_2$, and since $\lambda_1\neq\lambda_2$ the dot product must vanish.

Let $S\in\mathbb{R}^{n,n}$ be symmetric. Then:
- (i) all eigenvalues of $S$ are **real**;
- (ii) there exists an **orthogonal** matrix $P$ such that $$P^{-1}SP = P^\top S P = D$$ is diagonal, with the eigenvalues of $S$ on the diagonal.

Consequence: **every real symmetric matrix is diagonalizable**, and for every eigenvalue the algebraic multiplicity equals the geometric multiplicity.

"Symmetric" must mean **real** symmetric. The complex symmetric matrix $S=\begin{pmatrix}0&i\\ i&0\end{pmatrix}$ is symmetric but NOT real, and $\begin{pmatrix}1&i\\ i&-1\end{pmatrix}$ is symmetric yet not diagonalizable even over $\mathbb{C}$. The Spectral Theorem applies only to $S^\top=S$ with real entries.

blocks.title: Orthogonally diagonalizing a symmetric matrix S

steps.text: Find the eigenvalues from $\det(S-\lambda I)=0$ (all will be real).
For each eigenvalue compute an eigenvector basis of its eigenspace $\ker(S-\lambda I)$.
Eigenvectors from different eigenvalues are already orthogonal. Within a repeated eigenvalue's eigenspace, run Gram-Schmidt to make them orthogonal.
Normalize every eigenvector to unit length; these are the columns of $P$.
Then $P$ is orthogonal and $P^\top S P = D$, the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues (in the same column order).

blocks.title: A 2x2 symmetric matrix
blocks.text:
Take $A=\begin{pmatrix}5&2\\ 2&8\end{pmatrix}$. Characteristic polynomial: $(5-\lambda)(8-\lambda)-4=\lambda^2-13\lambda+36=(\lambda-9)(\lambda-4)$, so eigenvalues $9$ and $4$.

Eigenvectors: for $\lambda=9$, $v_1=(1,2)$; for $\lambda=4$, $v_2=(-2,1)$. Check $v_1\cdot v_2 = -2+2 = 0$ (as the Lemma guarantees). Normalize: $f_1=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt5}(1,2)$, $f_2=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt5}(-2,1)$.

Then $P=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt5}\begin{pmatrix}1&-2\\ 2&1\end{pmatrix}$ is orthogonal ($P^\top P=I_2$) and $P^{-1}AP=\begin{pmatrix}9&0\\ 0&4\end{pmatrix}$.

question.prompt: Eigenvectors $v_1,v_2$ of a real symmetric matrix belong to eigenvalues $3$ and $7$. What is $v_1\cdot v_2$?
question.explanation: Distinct eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix force their eigenvectors to be orthogonal, so $v_1\cdot v_2=0$. They need not be normalized, so B is wrong; the eigenvalues don't enter the dot product, so C and D are wrong.

blocks.text: The **orthogonal projection** of $u$ onto $v$ is $$\mathrm{pr}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|v\|^2}\,v.$$ The leftover $u-\mathrm{pr}_v(u)$ is orthogonal to $v$. This is the engine of Gram-Schmidt: subtract off the components along the vectors you already have.

blocks.caption: Given an ON set $\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}$ and a new independent vector $u$, this produces a unit vector orthogonal to all the $v_i$, extending the ON set.

blocks.title: Gram-Schmidt: ON basis of a span

steps.text: Subtract its projection from the second: $u_0 = u_2-\mathrm{pr}_{v_1}(u_2)$, then normalize $v_2=u_0/\|u_0\|$.
For each later generator $u_k$, subtract projections onto ALL previous $v_1,\dots,v_{k-1}$, then normalize.
If some $u_0$ comes out $=0$, that generator was dependent — discard it (the dimension is smaller).
Shortcut: if a new generator is already orthogonal to all previous $v_i$ (all dot products $0$), just normalize it directly.

blocks.text: You may rescale a vector by any positive constant before normalizing — e.g. replace $(\tfrac12,-\tfrac12,1,0)$ by $(1,-1,2,0)$ to avoid fractions. The final unit vector is the same. This keeps the arithmetic clean on the exam.

A **quadratic form** in $x_1,\dots,x_n$ is a homogeneous degree-2 polynomial $q(v)=v^\top A v=\sum_{i,j}a_{ij}x_ix_j$. We always take $A$ **symmetric**: the diagonal entry $a_{ii}$ is the coefficient of $x_i^2$, and the off-diagonal $a_{ij}=a_{ji}$ is **half** the coefficient of the mixed term $x_ix_j$.

blocks.title: Building the matrix of a form
blocks.text: For $q(x,y,z)=2x^2+y^2+5z^2+6xy+2yz$: diagonal $=2,1,5$; halve the mixed coefficients $6xy\to 3$, $2yz\to 1$, $0\,xz\to 0$. So $$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&3&0\\ 3&1&1\\ 0&1&5\end{pmatrix}.$$

blocks.tex: \text{classify } q \text{ by the signs of the eigenvalues } \lambda_i \text{ of its symmetric matrix } A
blocks.caption: Diagonalizing orthogonally turns $q$ into $\lambda_1 X_1^2+\cdots+\lambda_n X_n^2$, so the signs of the $\lambda_i$ decide everything.

blocks.text: A handy shortcut for the sign pattern without solving for eigenvalues: for a $2\times2$ symmetric $A$, positive definite $\iff a_{11}>0$ and $\det A>0$; indefinite $\iff \det A<0$ (eigenvalues of opposite sign). This is the leading-minors idea, but on this exam the eigenvalue signs are the official tool — always trust them.

question.prompt: A symmetric $3\times3$ matrix has eigenvalues $2,\,5,\,0$. Its quadratic form is:
question.explanation: All eigenvalues are $\ge 0$ but one is exactly $0$, so $q(v)\ge0$ always yet $q(v)=0$ for a nonzero eigenvector of the $0$ eigenvalue. That is positive semidefinite, not definite (A wrong). No negative eigenvalue, so not indefinite (C) or negative definite (D).

blocks.text: Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: if $p(x)=a_0+a_1x+\cdots+(-1)^nx^n$ is the characteristic polynomial of $A$, then $$p(A)=a_0I+a_1A+\cdots+(-1)^nA^n=0.$$ This lets you rewrite high powers $A^k$ and the inverse $A^{-1}$ as low-degree polynomials in $A$.

blocks.title: Inverse and a power via Cayley-Hamilton
blocks.text:
For $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\ 3&4\end{pmatrix}$, $p(x)=x^2-5x-2$, so $A^2-5A-2I=0$, i.e. $A^2=5A+2I$. From $2I=A^2-5A=A(A-5I)$ we get $A^{-1}=\tfrac12(A-5I)$.

For a matrix with $p(x)=(x-2)^2$ (so $A^2=4A-4I$): $A^3=A(4A-4I)=4A^2-4A=4(4A-4I)-4A=12A-16I$.

question.explanation: From $A^2-5A+2I=0$ we get $2I=5A-A^2=A(5I-A)$, so $A^{-1}=\tfrac12(5I-A)$ (option A). B drops the factor $\tfrac12$; C and D have the wrong sign.
